text: Current README (/Users/pablofernandez/src/tenex-edge/README.md) is dry, precise, proof-heavy, zero hype. Sample lines: "Citizenship for your agents" / "Verified live on relay.tenex.chat: a real opencode agent and a real codex agent each messaged a hub..." Product docs (docs/product-spec/vision.md, problem-space.md) are unusually self-aware and honest — they explicitly flag "the seductive demo we must not let define the project" re: the multi-agent-collision pain, and call it "acute for the dev power-user, narrow." IMPORTANT TENSION: the collision/clobbering framing (which is what your brief's product description leads with) is internally treated as the emotionally vivid but narrow hook — the deeper pitch is "durable identity + shared awareness across hosts" (amnesiac agents, re-explaining yourself every session). Recommend using collision-prevention as the top-of-funnel hook (it's what gets clicks) but let the README's proof and architecture carry the durable-identity story underneath — matches existing internal doctrine and avoids overclaiming. Vision doc's own language is reusable gold: "the human is the integration runtime," "a thousand isolated assistants re-introducing themselves forever," "the agent that helped you yesterday is a stranger today." [...] Triggers eye-rolls: "revolutionary," "game-changing," "seamless," "supercharge," "unlock," "next-generation," "AI-powered" as a standalone claim, orchestration-as-buzzword without a concrete mechanism, enterprise phrases ("enable synergy across your agentic workforce"), claiming to have solved trust/safety when you've only solved visibility. [...] Works: concrete verbs (see, mention, message, stop clobbering), specific proof ("a real opencode agent and a real codex agent messaged each other on relay.tenex.chat"), naming the exact host tools (Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode — not "AI assistants"), honest scoping/caveats (this crowd rewards "here's what this doesn't do yet"), terminal/code-block-first demonstration.
